court: High Court of Judicature at Madras
citation: Civil Miscellaneous Appeal No.2508 of 2008; M.P.No.1 of 2013

compensation_breakdown.interest: 7.5% per annum from the date of Claim Petition till the date of realisation

facts: On 05.10.2002 at about 1:45 PM, deceased Venkata Kondaiya was riding his Hero Honda motorcycle (Reg. No. TN-22 Y 1596) on Chennai-Minjur Road near Pungambedu bus stand when the lorry (Reg. No. TN-20 Z 3024) belonging to the 5th respondent coming from the opposite direction allegedly driven rashly and negligently collided with the motorcycle. As a result, Venkata Kondaiya sustained grievous injuries and died after being admitted to Stanley Government Hospital. At the time of accident he was employed as a Rivetter in the Electrical and Mechanical Department of Chennai Port Trust; claimants (wife, sons and mother) filed a claim petition seeking Rs.30,00,000/-.
arguments_claimant: Claimants argued that the driver held an endorsement in Ex.R1 authorising him to drive a 'transport vehicle' and therefore had a valid licence to drive the lorry. They contended the insurer could not repudiate liability on the basis of lack of endorsement and relied on authorities holding that minor or technical deviations in licence endorsement do not absolve insurers in third-party claims. Claimants urged confirmation of the Tribunal's finding on negligence and the awarded compensation.
arguments_respondent: The appellant-insurer contended the driver had licence only for 'light motor vehicle' and did not possess a valid licence/endorsement to drive a 'heavy goods vehicle' (the offending lorry), thus breaching the policy conditions and absolving the insurer of liability. The insurer relied on precedents where lack of licence or expired licences led to exoneration and argued pay-and-recover should not have been ordered here.
ratio_decidendi: "The mere fact that there was violation of the terms and conditions subject to which the insurance policy has been issued, cannot have the effect of exonerating the insurer from the statutory liability cast upon him in this regard to pay the amount to the third party victim." The court held that "the insurer has to prove that the insured was guilty of negligence and failed to exercise a reasonable care in the matter of fulfilling the conditions of the Policy regarding use of vehicle by duly licensed driver or one who was not disqualified to drive at the relevant time." Even if a breach is proved, "the insurer would not be allowed to avoid its liability towards the insured unless the said breach or breaches of the condition of driving licence is/are so fundamental as are found to have contributed to the cause of the accident." Applying these principles, because the owner remained ex parte and there was no evidence that the owner knew the licence was inadequate or failed to take reasonable care, the insurer remained liable to indemnify the third-party victim and was entitled only to recover from the insured thereafter.
final_order: Appeal dismissed; the Tribunal award of Rs.12,89,500/- is confirmed. The Appellant-Insurance Company is directed to pay Rs.12,89,500/- with interest at 7.5% p.a. from the date of the Claim Petition until realisation and may recover the amount from the 5th Respondent (owner) as per the mode indicated in Nanjappan's case.
summary: This High Court appeal arose from a motor accident on 05.10.2002 in which a heavy goods lorry hit a motorcycle, killing rider Venkata Kondaiya. The insurer contended the driver was not licensed to drive a heavy goods vehicle and sought to avoid liability; the Tribunal ordered the insurer to pay and recover. The Court held insurers remain liable to third parties under Chapter XI and may pay and later recover from the insured unless the breach of licence conditions was fundamental and causative; the Tribunal's award of Rs.12,89,500/- with 7.5% interest was confirmed. Practical effect: in third-party claims insurers must indemnify victims and then pursue remedy against owners/insured where appropriate.
embedding_summary: High Court of Madras, Civil Miscellaneous Appeal No.2508 of 2008 (judgment 22.10.2013): The Manager, The New India Assurance Co. Ltd. v. Chennamma & ors. Accident on 05.10.2002 near Pungambedu bus stand (Chennai-Minjur Road): lorry (TN-20 Z 3024) collided with motorcycle (TN-22 Y 1596) killing rider Venkata Kondaiya. Key legal issues: vehicle classification (gross vehicle weight 16,200 kg → 'heavy goods vehicle' under Section 2(16) M.V. Act), driver licence endorsement (licence for 'light motor vehicle' with 'transport vehicle' endorsement), insurer liability under Section 149(2)(a)(ii), and the 'pay and recover' doctrine in third-party claims. Court applied Swaran Singh, Lehru, Angad Kol, Nanjappan, S. Iyyapan and others, held insurer must indemnify the victim and may recover from insured; award of Rs.12,89,500/- confirmed with interest 7.5% p.a. (pay and recover directed against 5th respondent). Relevant statutes referenced: Section 2 definitions, Section 41, Section 149, Sections 146-149, Sections 163A/166, Section 174 and CMVR/notifications on vehicle types.
legal_principles: Pay-and-recover doctrine applies in third-party claims: insurer must indemnify victim and can later recover from insured
Insurer cannot avoid liability merely because of breach of policy conditions unless breach is fundamental and contributed to the accident
Onus is on insurer to prove breach of licence/ policy conditions and owner's negligence in entrusting the vehicle
Classification of a goods vehicle for licence purposes is based on gross vehicle weight for goods carriages (Section 2 definitions)
Owner must take reasonable care to verify licence on its face but is not required to investigate authenticity beyond reasonable satisfaction
cases_cited: National Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Swaran Singh, 2004 (1) TN MAC 104 (SC)
New India Assurance Co. Ltd. v. Manjit/Manjir Kaur and others, 2004 (1) TN MAC 210 (SC)
Ram Babu Tiwari v. United India Insurance Co. Ltd. and others, 2008 (2) TN MAC 347 (SC)
New India Assurance Co. Ltd. v. Roshanben Rahemansha Fakir and another, 2008 (2) TN MAC 201 (SC)
United India Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Lehru and others, (2003) 3 SCC 338
Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Angad Kol and others, 2009 (1) TN MAC 242 (SC) = 2009 SC 2151
United India Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Laxmi Narain Dhut, 2007 (1) TN MAC 301 (SC) = 2007 (4) SCALE 36
Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Shri Nanjappan and others, 2004 (1) TN MAC 211 (SC) = 2004 (2) CTC 464 (SC)
S. Iyyapan v. United India Insurance Company Limited and another, (2013) 7 SCC 62
United India Insurance Co. Ltd. v. E. Rajamanickam and another, 2011 (1) TN MAC 288
bm25_text: The Manager vs Chennamma High Court of Judicature at Madras 2013 On 05.10.2002 at about 1:45 PM, deceased Venkata Kondaiya was riding his Hero Honda motorcycle (Reg. No. TN-22 Y 1596) on Chennai-Minjur Road near Pungambedu bus stand when the lorry (Reg. No. TN-20 Z 3024) belonging to the 5th respondent coming from the opposite direction allegedly driven rashly and negligently collided with the motorcycle. As a result, Venkata Kondaiya sustained grievous injuries and died after being admitted to Stanley Government Hospital. At the time of accident he was employed as a Rivetter in the Electrical and Mechanical Department of Chennai Port Trust; claimants (wife, sons and mother) filed a claim petition seeking Rs.30,00,000/-. Claimants argued that the driver held an endorsement in Ex.R1 authorising him to drive a 'transport vehicle' and therefore had a valid licence to drive the lorry. They contended the insurer could not repudiate liability on the basis of lack of endorsement and relied on authorities holding that minor or technical deviations in licence endorsement do not absolve insurers in third-party claims. Claimants urged confirmation of the Tribunal's finding on negligence and the awarded compensation. The appellant-insurer contended the driver had licence only for 'light motor vehicle' and did not possess a valid licence/endorsement to drive a 'heavy goods vehicle' (the offending lorry), thus breaching the policy conditions and absolving the insurer of liability. The insurer relied on precedents where lack of licence or expired licences led to exoneration and argued pay-and-recover should not have been ordered here. "The mere fact that there was violation of the terms and conditions subject to which the insurance policy has been issued, cannot have the effect of exonerating the insurer from the statutory liability cast upon him in this regard to pay the amount to the third party victim." The court held that "the insurer has to prove that the insured was guilty of negligence and failed to exercise a reasonable care in the matter of fulfilling the conditions of the Policy regarding use of vehicle by duly licensed driver or one who was not disqualified to drive at the relevant time." Even if a breach is proved, "the insurer would not be allowed to avoid its liability towards the insured unless the said breach or breaches of the condition of driving licence is/are so fundamental as are found to have contributed to the cause of the accident." Applying these principles, because the owner remained ex parte and there was no evidence that the owner knew the licence was inadequate or failed to take reasonable care, the insurer remained liable to indemnify the third-party victim and was entitled only to recover from the insured thereafter. Appeal dismissed; the Tribunal award of Rs.12,89,500/- is confirmed. The Appellant-Insurance Company is directed to pay Rs.12,89,500/- with interest at 7.5% p.a. from the date of the Claim Petition until realisation and may recover the amount from the 5th Respondent (owner) as per the mode indicated in Nanjappan's case. This High Court appeal arose from a motor accident on 05.10.2002 in which a heavy goods lorry hit a motorcycle, killing rider Venkata Kondaiya. The insurer contended the driver was not licensed to drive a heavy goods vehicle and sought to avoid liability; the Tribunal ordered the insurer to pay and recover. The Court held insurers remain liable to third parties under Chapter XI and may pay and later recover from the insured unless the breach of licence conditions was fundamental and causative; the Tribunal's award of Rs.12,89,500/- with 7.5% interest was confirmed. Practical effect: in third-party claims insurers must indemnify victims and then pursue remedy against owners/insured where appropriate.
